title: Build a CI-Native Review Factory
date: 2026-06-21

How risk tiering drives the whole pipeline, how specialists fan out and a coordinator fuses, and why the model never decides which model runs."

import Mermaid from '../../../components/Mermaid.astro';

**Applies:** [Agent Infrastructure](/working-intel/topics/agent-infrastructure/) (the CI-native review factory), [Agent Design](/working-intel/topics/agent-design/) (specialist reviewers that specialize, scope, and fuse), and [Extensibility](/working-intel/topics/extensibility/) (open to extension, closed to modification). The infrastructure topic points at Cloudflare's review factory as the public example. This is a first-party one.

*Grounded in a working prototype (`@briggsd/code-reviewer`, a CI-native runner for GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket). Code is real and current; some excerpts are condensed for the page.*

## The one rule

A review runner has two kinds of work, and they want opposite things. Fetching diffs, filtering noise, scoring risk, enforcing timeouts, publishing comments, setting the CI status: that work has to be reliable, cheap, and the same every time. Judging whether a change is safe: that work needs a model. The whole design follows from keeping them apart.

> Deterministic code owns the boring, safety-critical parts. LLM agents handle the judgment: specialist reviewers fan out by risk and domain, a coordinator fuses their findings. The CI status is the merge gate; comments are just the human-facing UX.

<Mermaid
	title="Two layers: code orchestrates, the model judges"
	chart={`
flowchart TD
    CI["CI trigger · PR / MR"] --> ADP
    subgraph SHELL["deterministic shell · code owns this"]
        ADP["VCS adapter<br/>diff · metadata · prior state"] --> FILT["diff filter<br/>drop lockfiles · generated · vendored"]
        FILT --> RISK["risk classifier<br/>trivial · lite · full"]
        RISK --> CTX["context builder<br/>shared patches + metadata"]
    end
    CTX --> COORD
    subgraph AGENTS["agent layer · model owns judgment"]
        COORD["coordinator"] --> R1["code_quality"]
        COORD --> R2["security"]
        COORD --> R3["documentation"]
        COORD --> R4["performance"]
        COORD --> R5["release · compliance ·<br/>comprehension (opt-in)"]
        R1 --> FUSE["coordinator fuses<br/>dedupe · verify · decide"]
        R2 --> FUSE
        R3 --> FUSE
        R4 --> FUSE
        R5 --> FUSE
    end
    FUSE --> GND["evidence grounding<br/>demote unfound quotes"]
    GND --> PUB["publish summary + inline"]
    GND --> GATE{"CI gate · status is the merge gate"}
`}
/>

## Risk tiering is the deterministic spine

Everything downstream keys off one decision: how risky is this change? That decision is plain code, no model involved. It reads the diff and returns a tier.

```ts
// src/runner/risk-classifier.ts  (condensed)
export function classifyRisk(input: RiskClassificationInput): RiskAssessment {
  const sensitivePaths = input.diff.files
    .map((f) => f.path)
    .filter((p) => matchesAnyGlob(p, input.config.sensitivePaths));
  const reviewedFileCount = input.diff.files.length;
  const totalChangedLines = input.diff.totalAdditions + input.diff.totalDeletions;

  if (sensitivePaths.length > 0) return assess("full", "sensitive_paths");
  if (reviewedFileCount > 50 || totalChangedLines > 500) return assess("full", "large_change");
  if (reviewedFileCount <= 5 && totalChangedLines <= 25) return assess("trivial", "small_change");
  return assess("lite", "default_lite");
}
```

Touch an auth file or a migration and you get a full review regardless of size. That bypass is the point: the cheap path never applies to the changes that can hurt you.

The tier name then maps to behavior through a single declarative table. Reviewer selection, timeout scaling, tool policy, and the coordinator short-circuit all read from this one place instead of re-checking the tier themselves.

```ts
// src/runner/tier-profile.ts — the single source of truth
const TIER_PROFILES = Object.freeze({
  trivial: Object.freeze({
    reviewerRoleCap: ["code_quality"],          // only the fast role runs
    shortCircuitCoordinatorOnZeroFindings: true, // skip the coordinator if nothing's found
    timeoutScale: 0.25,
    denyContextTools: true,                      // no repo crawling on tiny diffs
    patchBudgetBytes: 64_000,
  }),
  lite: Object.freeze({
    reviewerRoleCap: "all_enabled",
    shortCircuitCoordinatorOnZeroFindings: true,
    timeoutScale: 0.5,
    denyContextTools: true,
    patchBudgetBytes: 512_000,
  }),
  full: Object.freeze({
    reviewerRoleCap: "all_enabled",
    shortCircuitCoordinatorOnZeroFindings: false, // a full review always synthesizes
    timeoutScale: 1,
    denyContextTools: false,                      // read tools allowed for deep review
    patchBudgetBytes: 4_000_000,
  }),
});
```

One table, and the cost profile of the entire run falls out of it: how many agents, how long they get, how much context they see, whether they can touch the repo. Change the economics of the system by editing data, not control flow.

## The agent layer: fan out, then fuse

Once the shell has picked a tier and built shared context, the selected specialist reviewers run concurrently. Each one is scoped to a single role (code quality, security, documentation, and performance, with release, compliance, and a comprehension gate as further roles), gets the same shared context by reference, and returns structured findings. Which roles run is itself tier-gated: a trivial diff gets only code quality, a full review gets the lot.

```ts
// src/runtime/pi-agent-runtime.ts — specialists run in parallel  (condensed)
const reviewerSettled = await Promise.allSettled(
  input.selectedReviewers.map(async (reviewer) => {
    const result = await this.runReviewer(reviewer);
    return { reviewer, result };
  }),
);
```

`allSettled`, not `all`: one reviewer timing out or erroring must not sink the others. Failures get classified and the coordinator is told what did not run, rather than the whole review crashing.

Then the coordinator fuses: dedupe across reviewers, verify the high-severity claims, and reduce everything to one decision and one CI status. Except when it does not have to.

<Mermaid
	title="Fan-out, fuse, and the short-circuit that skips the expensive step"
	chart={`
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as shell
    participant C as coordinator
    participant R as reviewers
    S->>S: classifyRisk() → tier
    S->>C: dispatch selected roles (timeout × scale)
    C->>R: run code_quality, security, ... (allSettled)
    R-->>C: findings[]
    alt trivial/lite tier · all succeeded · zero findings
        C-->>S: deterministic "approved" (skip synthesis)
    else has findings or full tier
        C->>C: fuse · dedupe · verify high-severity
        C-->>S: one decision + CI status
    end
`}
/>

The short-circuit is where determinism buys back money. If every reviewer succeeded and found nothing, on a low-risk tier there is nothing to synthesize, so the coordinator call is skipped entirely and a known-good summary is returned.

```ts
// src/runtime/pi-agent-runtime.ts — skip the coordinator when there's nothing to fuse
if (
  input.shortCircuitOnZeroFindings === true &&
  reviewerFailures.length === 0 &&
  reviewerResults.every((result) => result.findings.length === 0)
) {
  const summary = summarizeReview(input.context, []); // deterministic "approved"
  return { summary, reviewerResults, coordinatorShortCircuited: true };
}
```

Most PRs in a healthy repo are clean and small. The short-circuit means the common case never pays for the most expensive model call in the pipeline.

## Why the split is the whole point

Put the determinism around the model, not inside it. The model is good at judgment and bad at being a reliable system: it will not consistently fetch the right diff, respect a timeout, or fail closed. So the code does those things, and the contract handed to the agent is narrow: here is the scoped context, return findings in this shape. Three properties fall out of that boundary:

- **The model never decides which model runs.** Tier selection and roster are deterministic, so cost is bounded before a single token is spent.
- **Failures degrade, they don't crash.** A reviewer that dies is a missing input the coordinator notes, not a broken review. When synthesis itself fails, a deterministic fallback summary still ships.
- **The gate is code.** The CI status comes from the decision plus a `failOn` policy. Comments are the human UX on top; the merge gate underneath is not at the mercy of prose.

## Open to extension, never fork the core

The factory is also a worked example of [open to extension, closed to modification](/working-intel/topics/extensibility/), which is what lets it install once across many repos. Adopting repos never fork it; they configure per-repo through a small `.ai-review.json`. The parts that vary are registered units, not edits to the core:

- **Reviewers** are factory-owned `defineReviewer(...)` definitions. Adding the performance role was a new unit, not a new branch in the runner.
- **Version control** is an adapter contract. GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket are implementations behind one interface; a fourth host is a new adapter, not a rewrite.
- **Runtimes and providers** are registered and swappable, with a failback chain when a provider degrades.

The seam sits on the axis of variation (which hosts, which reviewers, which models) and the core stays flat. It is the same discipline that keeps the `TIER_PROFILES` table declarative: change behavior by adding data and units, not `if` branches. That flatness is also what keeps the system legible to the agents running inside it.

## Where it is, honestly

This is a working prototype, not a finished product. It runs real reviews today across GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, with risk tiering, evidence grounding (covered in a follow-up build), incremental re-review, and JSONL traces plus non-blocking telemetry. The rough edges are known: per-role model routing is not yet adopter-configurable, GitLab inline comments lag GitHub, and the self-evaluation harness exists but has no public feedback loop yet. The architecture, though, is the durable part: a deterministic shell with a narrow contract to the agents has held up as every other piece churned.
